sections.title: Peptic ulcer disease and gastric tumors

cards.front: A 4-week-old infant has projectile vomiting. What is the classic presentation of hypertrophic pyloric stenosis, and which single electrolyte/acid-base derangement is the exam answer?
cards.back: It presents around 3 to 6 weeks of age with projectile non-bilious vomiting, a palpable olive-shaped epigastric mass, and visible left-to-right peristalsis. The classic derangement is a hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis from loss of gastric HCl.

cards.front: How do you distinguish omphalocele from gastroschisis on the basis of location and whether a covering sac is present?
cards.back: Omphalocele is a midline defect where herniated viscera are covered by a sac and it is associated with other syndromes. Gastroschisis is a para-umbilical defect with no covering sac.

cards.front: In congenital diaphragmatic hernia, which side is usually affected, what is the most common cause of death, and how do Bochdalek and Morgagni defects differ in location?
cards.back: It is usually left-sided, and pulmonary hypoplasia is the most common cause of death. Bochdalek hernias are posterolateral while Morgagni hernias are anterior.

cards.front: What is the full clinical picture of hypertrophic pyloric stenosis, including sex predilection, age, vomiting character, and exam findings?
cards.back: It is 3 to 5 times more common in males and presents at 3 to 6 weeks with projectile non-bilious vomiting. Exam reveals a palpable olive and visible left-to-right peristalsis.

cards.front: In pyloric stenosis, what acid-base disturbance results, what is the initial diagnostic test, and what is the definitive treatment?
cards.back: It causes a hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis from loss of gastric acid. Ultrasound is the initial test, and a Ramstedt pyloromyotomy is the definitive treatment.

cards.front: Two named stress ulcers appear on every exam. Which one follows severe burns and which follows raised intracranial pressure or head injury, and what is the mechanism of each?
cards.back: Curling ulcers follow burns or shock and result from splanchnic hypoperfusion causing mucosal ischemia. Cushing ulcers follow raised intracranial pressure or head injury, where vagal stimulation drives gastric acid hypersecretion.

cards.front: What histologic feature distinguishes gastropathy from true acute gastritis, and which agents typically cause gastropathy?
cards.back: Gastropathy shows mucosal injury with an intact epithelium and absent inflammatory cells, classically from NSAIDs, alcohol, or bile. Gastritis is defined by neutrophil infiltration with erosion.

cards.front: Acute gastritis reflects an imbalance between damaging and defensive forces at the gastric mucosa. What makes up each side, and how do NSAIDs tip the balance?
cards.back: Damaging forces (acid and pepsin) overwhelm defensive forces (mucus, bicarbonate, mucosal blood flow, and prostaglandins). NSAIDs inhibit prostaglandin synthesis, stripping away a key mucosal defense.

cards.front: Contrast Curling and Cushing stress ulcers by their clinical trigger and underlying mechanism.
cards.back: Curling ulcers follow burns or shock and arise from mucosal ischemia due to hypovolemia. Cushing ulcers follow raised intracranial pressure, where vagal stimulation causes gastric acid hypersecretion.

cards.front: Chronic gastritis is divided into types you must distinguish. How do H. pylori and autoimmune gastritis differ in their location, effect on acid secretion, and major complication?
cards.back: H. pylori gastritis is antral-predominant, can raise or maintain acid, and risks MALT lymphoma and adenocarcinoma. Autoimmune gastritis affects the fundus and body, causes hypochlorhydria and B12 deficiency with pernicious anemia, and spares the antrum.

cards.front: For H. pylori gastritis, what is the predominant location, key virulence factor, diagnostic stain, preferred biopsy site, and first-line eradication regimen?
cards.back: It is antral-predominant and the CagA virulence factor is important; the organism is seen with a Warthin-Starry stain on an antral biopsy. First-line triple therapy is a PPI plus clarithromycin and amoxicillin.

cards.front: Chronic H. pylori infection is a class 1 carcinogen. Which two gastric malignancies is it most associated with?
cards.back: Chronic H. pylori gastritis predisposes to gastric MALT lymphoma and gastric adenocarcinoma.

cards.front: What autoantibodies define autoimmune chronic gastritis, what acid and hematologic consequences follow, and which part of the stomach does it affect versus spare?
cards.back: It is driven by anti-parietal cell and anti-intrinsic factor antibodies, producing hypochlorhydria and B12 deficiency with pernicious anemia. It targets the fundus and corpus and spares the antrum.

cards.front: Ménétrier disease is a hypertrophic gastropathy. What is its characteristic morphology, acid status, and major clinical complication?
cards.back: It shows foveolar hyperplasia with corkscrew glands and giant gastric folds, accompanied by hypochlorhydria. The hallmark complication is a protein-losing gastroenteropathy. It is unrelated to Ménière ear disease.

cards.front: Duodenal and gastric ulcers differ in how meals affect pain and which artery each threatens. What are the two distinctions flagged as very important?
cards.back: Duodenal ulcer pain improves with meals, whereas gastric ulcer pain worsens with meals. A posterior duodenal ulcer can erode the gastroduodenal artery, while a gastric ulcer on the lesser curvature can erode the left gastric artery.

cards.front: Compare the typical sites and H. pylori association of duodenal versus gastric ulcers, and how do benign and malignant ulcers differ on gross morphology?
cards.back: Duodenal ulcers make up about 90%, sit anteriorly near the pylorus, and are over 95% H. pylori-related; gastric ulcers favor the lesser curvature. Benign ulcers look punched-out with clean margins, while malignant ulcers have heaped-up margins.

cards.front: What is the meal-pain rule that distinguishes duodenal from gastric ulcers?
cards.back: Duodenal ulcer pain improves with meals, classically returning a few hours later. Gastric ulcer pain worsens with meals.

cards.front: Which arteries are threatened by a posterior duodenal ulcer versus a gastric lesser-curvature ulcer, and what complication results from kissing ulcers?
cards.back: A posterior duodenal ulcer can erode the gastroduodenal artery, causing emergent hemorrhage, while a gastric or lesser-curvature ulcer threatens the left gastric artery. Kissing ulcers (anterior and posterior) can lead to gastric outlet obstruction.

cards.front: Gastric adenocarcinoma divides into two Lauren types. How do the intestinal and diffuse types differ in genetics, morphology, and the signature cell type?
cards.back: The intestinal type (~90%) is associated with APC mutations and intestinal metaplasia, occurs distally, and forms glands. The diffuse type (~10%) is driven by CDH1/E-cadherin loss, features signet-ring cells, and can produce linitis plastica.

cards.front: Among gastric polyps, which type is most common and benign, which is associated with APC, and which is premalignant, and what size raises that risk?
cards.back: Hyperplastic and inflammatory polyps account for about 90% and are benign, while fundic gland polyps are linked to APC. Adenomatous polyps make up roughly 10% and are premalignant, with risk rising above 2 cm.

cards.front: Detail the Lauren classification of gastric adenocarcinoma, contrasting the intestinal and diffuse types by frequency, genetics, morphology, and gross appearance.
cards.back: The intestinal type is about 90%, linked to APC and intestinal metaplasia, distal in location, and gland-forming. The diffuse type is about 10%, driven by CDH1/E-cadherin loss, composed of signet-ring cells, and can present as linitis plastica.

cards.front: What are the classic named metastatic and paraneoplastic signs of gastric carcinoma, and what does each represent?
cards.back: Virchow node is a left supraclavicular metastasis, Sister Mary Joseph nodule is a periumbilical metastasis, and Krukenberg tumor is bilateral ovarian metastasis from signet-ring cells. Trousseau sign is migratory thrombophlebitis as a paraneoplastic feature.

cards.front: Contrast gastric MALT lymphoma and GIST by their characteristic genetics, cell of origin, and immunohistochemical markers.
cards.back: Gastric MALT lymphoma carries t(11;18) or t(14;18), expresses CD19 and CD20, and can transform into diffuse large B-cell lymphoma. GIST arises from interstitial cells of Cajal, harbors KIT or PDGFRA mutations, and stains positive for CD117 and DOG1.
